regions/ucayali_region.json.name: Ucayali Region
regions/ucayali_region.json.history: Ucayali Region, located in eastern Peru within the Amazon basin, has been inhabited for thousands of years by indigenous groups including the Shipibo-Konibo, Ashaninka, and other native peoples who developed sophisticated knowledge of the rainforest. Spanish colonization in the 16th and 17th centuries brought significant disruption to these communities, though many maintained their cultural practices in remote areas. The region experienced economic booms during the rubber extraction era of the late 19th and early 20th centuries, which brought both wealth and exploitation to the territory. In modern times, Ucayali has developed as a center for agriculture, timber, and river commerce while facing ongoing pressures from deforestation and environmental change.
regions/ucayali_region.json.culturalNotes: Ucayali is defined by its profound Amazonian identity, where indigenous traditions remain central to daily life and spiritual practice across numerous communities. The Shipibo-Konibo people are particularly renowned for their intricate textile designs and ceramic work, which reflect complex geometric patterns and cosmological knowledge passed down through generations. The region's character is shaped by its dependence on the Ucayali River and rainforest resources, creating a culture deeply connected to natural cycles, traditional medicine, and communal social structures.
regions/ucayali_region.json.bestTimeToVisit: Visit during the dry season from June to September for easier river travel and wildlife observation.
regions/ucayali_region.json.languageNotes: While Spanish is the official language, Ucayali is home to numerous indigenous languages including Shipibo-Konibo, Ashaninka, and others spoken by native communities. Visitors will find Spanish widely used in urban areas like Pucallpa, though learning basic indigenous words and phrases shows respect for local cultures and can enrich interaction with indigenous peoples throughout the region.

places.name: Pucallpa
places.description: The capital and largest city of Ucayali Region, serving as the gateway to the Amazon rainforest and a major hub for river commerce. It offers indigenous markets, wildlife centers, and is the primary departure point for jungle expeditions and river cruises.

activities.detail: Multi-day or day-trip boat expeditions along the Amazon's major tributary to spot pink river dolphins, caimans, and macaws in their natural habitat.
Walk through open-air aviaries filled with thousands of native Amazonian butterflies and visit the adjoining small zoo with rescued jungle animals.
The main central square featuring the cathedral, local markets, and colonial-era architecture where you can observe daily Pucallpan life and street vendors.
Browse authentic crafts, textiles, and indigenous artwork directly from Shipibo-Konibo and other local communities in open-air and covered market stalls.
Navigate suspended bridges and platforms high above the forest floor near lodges outside the city to observe rainforest ecology and wildlife from above.
Learn about Ucayali's pre-Columbian history, indigenous cultures, and natural history through displays of ceramics, textiles, and anthropological artifacts.

food.detail: Mashed plantains mixed with butter and local cheese, typically served as a breakfast side or light meal that represents Amazonian comfort food.
A pyramid-shaped packet of rice, chicken, eggs, and olives wrapped and cooked in banana leaves, traditionally eaten during religious festivals but available year-round.
Fresh white fish (paiche is a giant Amazonian river fish) cured in lime juice, onions, and peppers, showcasing the region's river bounty.
A hearty broth made with local river fish, plantains, and regional vegetables served as a warming lunch or dinner staple.
Tender white hearts harvested from native palm trees, served fresh with onions and lime as a local delicacy highlighting sustainable jungle produce.

culture.detail: Many locals and businesses operate around river transport times, so plan activities around dock schedules and water levels that affect boat availability.
The indigenous Shipibo-Konibo people are prominent in Pucallpa; show respect when visiting markets or ceremonies, ask permission before photographing, and support artisans directly.
Dengue and malaria are present year-round; use insect repellent consistently, wear long sleeves at dawn and dusk, and consult health advisories before travel.

While some lodge operators speak English, Spanish is essential for navigating markets, restaurants, and independent exploration; indigenous languages are spoken in communities but not by most urban residents.

places.name: Yarinacocha Lake
places.description: A stunning oxbow lake just outside Pucallpa, surrounded by lush rainforest and home to diverse wildlife including pink river dolphins, caimans, and tropical birds. This is the best spot near the city for immersive jungle experiences and indigenous community visits.

activities.detail: Take an early morning or sunset boat tour to observe pink river dolphins (boto) hunting and playing in the lake's calm waters.
Visit nearby Shipibo villages to learn about traditional textile weaving, pottery, and indigenous plant knowledge directly from community members.
Join a guided night boat tour with a spotlight to see black caimans and caiman eyes reflecting in the darkness along the shoreline.
Explore the rainforest canopy surrounding the lake to spot macaws, herons, kingfishers, and other tropical bird species.
Relax on the sandy beaches at the lake's edge and swim in designated safe areas with views of forested shorelines.
Participate in traditional plant medicine ceremonies led by experienced shamans at retreats located near the lake.
Experience elevated walkways through the rainforest canopy to observe wildlife and forest ecology from a treetop perspective.

food.detail: A hearty stewed fish dish made with freshly caught lake fish, tomatoes, onions, and regional spices, served with rice and plantains.
Fresh river shrimp cured in lime juice with cilantro, onions, and local peppers, a refreshing appetizer reflecting Amazonian seafood traditions.
A traditional Amazonian rice and meat dish wrapped in plantain leaves and boiled, often made with chicken or fish and served during celebrations.
Mashed fried plantains mixed with cheese and served alongside chorizo, a savory regional breakfast staple found throughout the area.

food.text: Ayahuasca Tea and Plant Medicine Dieta
food.detail: Traditional herbal preparations and diet foods consumed as part of shamanic retreats, often including simple vegetable broths and specific jungle plants.
A fermented corn drink prepared traditionally by indigenous communities and served at local celebrations and gatherings.

culture.detail: Always ask permission before photographing indigenous people or entering villages, and consider purchasing crafts directly to support local economies.
The lake region experiences heavy rain from November to March; plan visits during drier months (June to September) for better boat access and activities.
While some English is spoken at tourist lodges, learning basic Spanish phrases greatly enhances interactions with local boat operators and community members.
Indigenous cultures and wildlife are most active at dawn and dusk; book early morning and evening tours to experience the lake's richest moments and respect natural rhythms.

places.name: Contamana
places.description: A remote riverside town upriver from Pucallpa known for authentic Amazon culture, indigenous Shipibo communities, and access to pristine rainforest. It offers a more authentic and less touristy experience than Pucallpa with excellent wildlife viewing opportunities.

activities.detail: Spend time in nearby Shipibo villages to learn about indigenous weaving traditions, ayahuasca ceremonies, and daily riverside life directly from community members.
Take guided boat trips along the Ucayali River at dawn or dusk to observe pink river dolphins, caimans, anacondas, and tropical bird species in their natural habitat.
Trek into primary and secondary rainforest with local guides to discover medicinal plants, giant trees, poison dart frogs, and lesser-known wildlife away from main tourist routes.
Explore the local market to see fresh produce, fish, indigenous crafts, and interact with residents in an authentic, non-touristy setting.
Participate in traditional ayahuasca ceremonies led by experienced shamans in Shipibo communities, offering spiritual and therapeutic experiences under proper guidance.
Visit nearby oxbow lakes formed by the Ucayali River's meanders to paddling, bird watching, and see how local communities fish in these isolated water systems.

food.detail: Grilled or stewed giant river fish caught fresh from the Ucayali, often prepared with plantains and indigenous seasonings for an authentic Amazonian main course.
Mashed plantains mixed with pork fat and served alongside cured, smoked pork strips—a hearty traditional Ucayali region dish.
Fresh river shrimp ceviche prepared with lime juice, onions, and local chilies, reflecting the region's abundant freshwater crustaceans.

food.text: Shipibo Medicinal Brew (Chuchuhuasi or Sangre de Grado)
food.detail: Traditional herbal remedies made from jungle plants, used both as tonics and in ceremonies, available at local markets and from indigenous vendors.
Hearts of palm harvested from the rainforest, served fresh or in salads with local vegetables—a delicacy unique to Amazonian regions.
A warming soup combining cassava root with local piranha fish and regional spices, representing authentic Contamana cooking.

culture.detail: Never photograph or record ayahuasca ceremonies or Shipibo rituals without explicit permission, and approach sacred sites with reverence and humility.

culture.text: Learn Basic Spanish and Shipibo Greetings
culture.detail: While Spanish is spoken, learning simple Shipibo words like 'Aní' (hello) and showing genuine interest in the language demonstrates respect to indigenous communities.
Always hire local licensed guides for jungle and river activities—the environment is genuinely dangerous and guides provide essential knowledge about wildlife behavior and weather patterns.
When visiting Shipibo communities, bring small practical gifts like school supplies, soap, or tools rather than money, as this shows cultural respect and direct support.

places.name: Puerto Inca
places.description: A small port town on the Ucayali River where the Peruvian Highway connects to river transport, serving as a strategic point for exploring both the southern Amazon and accessing riverine communities. It's a working river port with authentic river culture and a gateway to remote jungle areas.

activities.detail: Take guided motorboat excursions upriver or downriver to spot river dolphins, caimans, and bird species while experiencing the working rhythm of the river.

activities.text: Visit local river ports and timber operations
activities.detail: Observe the authentic working port where timber, fish, and agricultural goods are loaded and unloaded, offering insight into the region's economic lifeblood.

activities.text: Community visits to nearby indigenous settlements
activities.detail: Arrange visits to Shipibo or other indigenous communities accessible by boat to learn traditional crafts, agriculture, and river-based livelihoods.
Join local fishermen or hire a guide for piranhas, catfish, and other Amazonian species in safe, productive stretches of the Ucayali.
Browse the informal marketplace where jungle goods, fresh produce, and handcrafts from upriver communities converge with highway commerce.

activities.text: Night jungle walks with local guides
activities.detail: Trek into nearby forest edges after dark to spot nocturnal wildlife, insects, and hear the soundscape of the Amazon.

food.detail: A regional favorite of mashed fried plantains mixed with chorizo and melted fresh cheese, reflecting the blend of riverside and Andean influences.
A tamale-like package of rice, chicken, and olives wrapped in banana leaves, a staple feast food sold by local vendors and in simple eateries.
Ceviche prepared with that morning's catch from the Ucayali, typically dorado or catfish, served with plantain and yuca.
A local delicacy harvested from fallen palms, served fresh or in mixed salads at small comedores reflecting jungle ingredients.
A hearty broth featuring the abundant piranha caught in the river, often cooked with local root vegetables and served in working-class restaurants.

food.text: Fresh açaí bowls and jungle fruits
food.detail: Local vendors sell açaí, camu camu, and other Amazonian fruits blended or served fresh, reflecting the region's biodiversity.

culture.detail: Water levels, weather, and boat availability are unpredictable; plan flexibility into your schedule and communicate openly with local guides about safe travel windows.
Terms like 'río', 'bote', 'maderería', and greetings to boatmen will deepen interactions and show respect for the working culture.

culture.text: Dress practically for humidity and insects
culture.detail: Wear light, long sleeves, closed shoes, and carry insect repellent; the climate is hot and humid, and mosquitoes and other insects are persistent.

culture.text: Ask permission before photographing people or communities
culture.detail: Locals and especially indigenous peoples may object to photos; always ask first and be prepared to respect refusals or negotiate a small contribution.

places.name: Atalaya
places.description: A frontier settlement at the confluence of the Ucayali and Tambo rivers, marking the eastern edge of accessible Amazon tourism in Peru. It offers access to indigenous territories, pristine rainforest, and serves as a base for extended jungle expeditions.

activities.detail: Stand at the meeting point of the Ucayali and Tambo rivers to witness the dramatic convergence of two major waterways in the Amazon basin.
Hire local guides to venture into primary rainforest around Atalaya in search of wildlife, medicinal plants, and indigenous communities.
Take multi-day canoe trips upriver on the Tambo to reach remote indigenous settlements and pristine forest areas inaccessible by land.
Arrange respectful visits to nearby Ashaninka indigenous territories to learn traditional practices, crafts, and forest knowledge directly from community members.
Join evening canoe trips to observe caimans, pink river dolphins, and nocturnal jungle creatures along the river edges.
Browse Atalaya's small market for fresh tropical fruits, indigenous handicrafts, and locally-caught fish brought in by river traders.

food.detail: A massive freshwater fish native to the Amazon, grilled whole or filleted and served simply salted, showcasing the river's bounty.
A traditional Peruvian jungle dish of rice, chicken, olives, and egg wrapped in plantain leaves and boiled, commonly eaten at celebrations.
Local white fish cured in lime juice with cilantro and onions, prepared fresh daily using that morning's catch from the Ucayali.
Unleavened cassava flatbread made by indigenous communities, often served with wild jungle honey as a staple carbohydrate source.
Mashed plantains mixed with butter and cheese, formed into patties and fried, a hearty Amazonian comfort food.

culture.detail: Indigenous community members, especially the Ashaninka, may object to being photographed without consent due to cultural beliefs and past exploitation.
Always use established local guides who understand river safety, seasonal conditions, and maintain respectful relationships with indigenous groups.
River conditions and wildlife visibility vary dramatically by season; dry season (May-September) offers better access, while wet season floods restrict travel routes.

culture.text: Learn basic Quechua or Ashaninka phrases
culture.detail: A few words of local indigenous languages show respect and facilitate genuine connection with community members who may speak limited Spanish.

places.name: Shipibo Indigenous Villages
places.description: Traditional communities around Pucallpa and along the Ucayali River where the Shipibo people maintain their ancestral customs, crafts, and knowledge of medicinal plants. Visiting these villages provides authentic cultural immersion and the opportunity to purchase direct traditional textiles and ceramics.

activities.detail: Watch Shipibo women create traditional geometric patterned textiles using natural dyes and ancestral weaving techniques passed down through generations.
Learn to hand-craft traditional Shipibo pottery with intricate pre-Columbian designs under the guidance of master artisans in village workshops.
Explore village gardens and forest areas with local guides who identify and explain the traditional uses of Amazonian medicinal plants in Shipibo healing practices.
Participate in traditional plant medicine ceremonies led by experienced shamans, though these should be approached with respect and careful selection of legitimate practitioners.
Take dugout canoe trips along the Ucayali River with local boatmen to observe wildlife, visit remote settlements, and experience daily river life.
Browse and purchase directly from artisans selling handmade textiles, ceramics, beadwork, and carved wooden items at village gathering points near Pucallpa.
Attend evening cultural presentations featuring Shipibo songs, dances, and instrumental music that tell stories of ancestral traditions and daily village life.

food.detail: Fish wrapped in banana leaves and cooked over open flames, seasoned with local spices and often served with plantains and cassava root.
A pyramid-shaped packet of rice, chicken, and olives wrapped in bijao leaves and boiled, traditionally eaten during celebrations and special occasions.
A unique Amazonian twist on ceviche using cassava root instead of fish, mixed with citrus juice and local herbs for a distinctive vegetable-based dish.
A hearty peanut soup thickened with ground peanuts and containing plantain and local vegetables, reflecting Shipibo comfort food traditions.
Mashed fried plantains served alongside smoked or salted dried meat, a staple protein dish that sustains villagers during river work and daily activities.
Fresh açaí berries from local sources served as bowls topped with granola and regional fruits, increasingly available in village visitor areas near Pucallpa.

culture.detail: Always request permission before photographing people, ceremonies, or sacred objects, and understand that some spiritual practices are not open to outsiders regardless of payment.
Using simple Shipibo phrases like 'Osí' (hello) and 'Opa' (thank you) demonstrates respect and genuine interest in the culture beyond tourism.
Buy crafts and textiles straight from makers rather than middlemen to ensure fair compensation reaches the families who created the work and maintained traditions.

culture.text: Dress Modestly and Respect Sacred Items
culture.detail: Wear respectful clothing that covers shoulders and knees, avoid touching ceremonial objects or altars without permission, and follow local guides' cultural protocols.

places.name: Aguaytía
places.description: A frontier town northwest of Pucallpa serving as a hub for exploring northern Ucayali and the transition zone between highlands and Amazon. It provides access to lesser-visited rainforest areas and serves as a departure point for overland jungle routes.

activities.detail: Navigate the main river by dugout canoe to spot pink river dolphins, caimans, and macaws in their natural habitat.
Trek to nearby indigenous settlements to learn about traditional weaving, plant medicine, and Amazonian forest ecology directly from Shipibo communities.
Stay at basic but authentic lodges deeper in the forest surrounding Aguaytía to experience nocturnal wildlife and guided night walks.
Join or organize multiday hikes following old logging trails and forest paths toward the Peruvian-Brazilian frontier region.
Browse the central market for fresh rainforest fruits, medicinal plants, and local crafts made by Ucayali artisans.
Fish for piranha and other river species with local guides using traditional methods in quieter tributaries.

food.detail: A wrapped packet of rice, chicken, and olives cooked in plantain leaves, a hearty staple throughout the Ucayali region.
Mashed plantains mixed with pork lard and served with fresh local cheese, a comforting breakfast or lunch dish.
Thick, meaty fillets of paiche (a giant Amazonian fish) grilled simply with lime and local herbs.
A light, bland diet (typically rice, plantain, fish broth) prepared before or after participating in traditional plant medicine ceremonies.
Brothy soups made from catfish or other daily catches, flavored with yucca, local greens, and forest tubers.

culture.detail: Always ask permission and hire local guides when visiting Shipibo or other native communities; never photograph people or sacred spaces without consent.
Aguaytía moves slowly; expect limited electricity, intermittent water, and travel delays—arrive with patience and flexibility rather than fixed schedules.
While Spanish is spoken, many locals and guides also speak Shipibo; learning a few phrases or using a translator shows respect and improves the experience.
From November to April, jungle roads become muddy and rivers swell; plan activities accordingly and pack waterproof gear essential year-round.

places.name: Ucayali River
places.description: The major river system flowing through the region, crucial for transportation and offering boat tours with excellent wildlife viewing including pink dolphins and caimans. River cruises provide the best way to experience the Amazon's ecology and visit remote communities.

activities.detail: Early morning boat tours along the Ucayali offer the best chances to see endangered pink river dolphins (boto) surfacing and hunting fish in their natural habitat.

activities.text: Canopy Walkway at Manu National Park
activities.detail: Multi-day jungle expeditions from the Ucayali access primary rainforest canopy platforms where you can observe macaws, harpy eagles, and howler monkeys at treetop level.
Evening boat cruises using spotlights reveal black caimans and spectacled caimans along the riverbanks, a thrilling nocturnal wildlife experience.
Day trips to Shipibo villages along the river allow you to purchase traditional textiles, learn about ayahuasca shamanic practices, and observe intricate geometric pottery designs.
Small motorboat excursions to nearby oxbow lakes like Laguna Contamana feature calm waters perfect for spotting river otters, anacondas, and rare water birds.
Rustic eco-lodges accessible only by river offer guided morning birdwatching walks, medicinal plant tours, and sunset observation decks overlooking the Ucayali.

food.detail: A beloved regional dish of mashed plantains mixed with pork crackling, formed into patties and served with local melted cheese.
A hearty broth made with river fish caught fresh daily, yams, and local spices, commonly served in river communities.
An Amazonian specialty of rice, eggs, and meat wrapped in banana leaves and boiled, traditionally eaten during festivals and special occasions.
Fresh white fish from the Ucayali prepared Peruvian-style with lime juice, chilies, and served with crispy plantain chips and yuca.
Fresh açaí berries harvested locally are blended into bowls topped with granola and fresh fruits, a refreshing Amazonian superfood treat.

culture.detail: Ayahuasca and shamanic practices are sacred to indigenous communities; approach with genuine respect and only participate if invited by legitimate practitioners.
Small settlements rely on the river economy; greet locals respectfully, ask permission before photographing people or homes, and purchase directly from artisans rather than middlemen.
The Ucayali's water levels rise significantly December through May, making some areas inaccessible; plan visits June through November for optimal wildlife viewing and boat access.
While Spanish is spoken, Shipibo and other indigenous languages dominate remote areas; learning basic Spanish greetings shows respect and enhances community interactions.

regions.name: Cusco Region

regions.name: Puno Region

regions.name: Arequipa Region
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from May to September for clear mountain views and perfect conditions for volcano trekking.

regions.name: Amazon Region
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit during the dry season from June to October for better wildlife viewing and easier river navigation.

regions.name: Central Highlands
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from April to October during the dry season for mountain exploration and cultural experiences.

regions.name: North Coast
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from December to March for beach weather, though the region is pleasant year-round.

regions.name: North Highlands
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from May to September for dry weather and exploration of colonial architecture and natural springs.

regions.name: Ucayali Region

top10.name: Lima
top10.description: Peru's vibrant capital and largest city, Lima is a world-class culinary destination and cultural hub with museums, colonial architecture, and modern neighborhoods. It's essential for understanding Peruvian history, art, and cuisine, and serves as the gateway to most Peruvian travel.

top10.name: Sacred Valley
top10.description: A lush valley between Cusco and Machu Picchu dotted with Incan ruins, traditional villages, and agricultural terraces. It offers stunning landscapes, authentic culture, and archaeological sites less crowded than Machu Picchu itself.

top10.name: Lake Titicaca
top10.description: The world's highest navigable lake at 3,812 meters, straddling Peru and Bolivia with breathtaking mountain views and rich indigenous culture. It's the heart of Puno region and offers boat tours, island visits, and stunning sunsets.

top10.name: Nazca Lines
top10.description: Massive pre-Columbian geoglyphs etched into the desert floor, visible only from above, depicting animals and geometric shapes of mysterious origin and purpose. They're among archaeology's greatest mysteries and best viewed by small aircraft tour.

top10.name: Colca Canyon
top10.description: One of the world's deepest canyons, twice as deep as the Grand Canyon, offering stunning trekking routes and opportunities to see Andean condors soaring overhead. The dramatic landscape and indigenous terraced agriculture create an unforgettable experience.

top10.name: Iquitos
top10.description: The largest city in the Peruvian Amazon and the gateway to the rainforest, built on the banks of the Río Amazonas. It's the primary hub for jungle lodges, river cruises, and expeditions into remote areas of the Amazon.

top10.name: Trujillo
top10.description: Peru's third-largest city and the cultural heart of the North Coast, featuring stunning colonial architecture and excellent museums dedicated to pre-Columbian civilizations. It serves as the main hub for exploring the region's archaeological treasures and offers quality restaurants, hotels, and vibrant local culture.

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 40 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: National public holiday marking the start of the new calendar year with celebrations across Peru.

festivals.name: Marinera Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Peru's national dance the Marinera with competitions and performances along the northern coast.

festivals.name: Fiesta de San Sebastián
festivals.description: Traditional festival honoring Saint Sebastian with colorful processions, dance, and local music in the Cusco highlands.

festivals.name: Carnaval Amazónico
festivals.description: Vibrant carnival celebration in the Amazon basin featuring water battles, music, and indigenous traditions.

festivals.name: Carnival of Puno
festivals.description: Colorful pre-Lenten carnival featuring traditional dances, elaborate costumes, and Andean music traditions.

festivals.name: Carnival of Cajamarca
festivals.description: Traditional highland carnival with water throwing, parades, and colorful local costumes celebrating regional identity.

festivals.name: Fiesta de la Candelaria
festivals.description: One of South America's most spectacular religious festivals blending Catholic and indigenous traditions with elaborate processions and dance competitions.

festivals.name: Día de la Independencia
festivals.description: National holiday celebrating Peru's independence from Spanish colonial rule with patriotic events and festivities nationwide.

festivals.name: Corpus Christi
festivals.description: Religious festival featuring processions of saints and sacred relics through the streets of Cusco with indigenous spiritual elements.

festivals.name: Inti Raymi
festivals.description: Spectacular recreation of the Inca winter solstice festival with thousands of performers in traditional costumes at Sacsayhuamán fortress.

festivals.name: Qoyllur Rit'i
festivals.description: Ancient indigenous pilgrimage to a glacier in the Andes where thousands gather for spiritual ceremonies and to witness the 'star snow'.

festivals.name: San Juan Festival
festivals.description: Amazonian celebration of Saint John with bonfires, traditional music, and indigenous ceremonies honoring water and fertility.

festivals.name: Festival of the Virgin of Carmen
festivals.description: Religious festival honoring the patron saint with processions, traditional dances like the Paucartambo dance, and indigenous ceremonies.

festivals.name: Festival of San Santiago
festivals.description: Celebration honoring Saint James with traditional dances, bullfights, and regional festivities along the northern coast.

festivals.name: Assumption of Mary
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating the Assumption of the Virgin Mary with religious observances and community gatherings.

festivals.name: Fiesta Patria
festivals.description: National battle commemoration celebrating Peru's military victory with parades and patriotic ceremonies.

festivals.name: All Saints' Day
festivals.description: Religious observance where Peruvians honor deceased loved ones with visits to gravesites and traditional foods.

festivals.name: Día de Muertos Market
festivals.description: Highland celebration where families prepare special foods and offerings for deceased relatives with indigenous spiritual traditions.

festivals.name: Immaculate Conception
festivals.description: National public holiday and religious festival celebrating the Immaculate Conception of Mary with processions and observances.

festivals.name: Santuranticuy
festivals.description: Ancient Quechua Christmas tradition in Cusco's plaza where artisans sell religious figurines and handicrafts for nativity scenes.

festivals.name: Christmas Day
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating the birth of Jesus Christ with religious services and family gatherings throughout Peru.

festivals.name: Festival of San Pedro y San Pablo
festivals.description: Coastal celebration honoring the patron saints with colorful processions, fishing traditions, and regional music and dance.

festivals.name: Arequipa Foundation Day
festivals.description: Regional celebration of Arequipa's founding with parades, cultural events, and festivities showcasing the city's heritage.

festivals.name: Huamanga Festival
festivals.description: Festival celebrating Peruvian artisan crafts with displays of textiles, ceramics, and traditional crafts from the central highlands.

festivals.name: Holy Week in Ayacucho
festivals.description: One of Peru's most important religious celebrations featuring elaborate processions, palm ceremonies, and traditional highland religious observances.

festivals.name: Fiesta de San Francisco de Asís
festivals.description: Religious celebration honoring Saint Francis of Assisi with masses and religious processions in Lima and other regions.

festivals.name: Peruano Festival of Colors
festivals.description: Vibrant cultural festival in Lima celebrating Peruvian art, music, dance, and crafts with performances and exhibitions.

festivals.name: Mistura Food Festival
festivals.description: Premier gastronomic festival showcasing Peru's diverse cuisine with famous chefs, cooking demonstrations, and tasting experiences.

festivals.description: Religious festival featuring traditional boat processions on Lake Titicaca and indigenous dances honoring the Virgin of Carmen.

festivals.description: Festival celebrating traditional Andean textile techniques with workshops, exhibitions, and demonstrations by indigenous weavers.

festivals.name: Fiesta del Chocolate
festivals.description: Festival celebrating Peruvian cacao and chocolate with tastings, chocolate-making demonstrations, and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Lima Fashion Week
festivals.description: International fashion event showcasing contemporary Peruvian designers and traditional textile artisans on the runway.

festivals.description: Religious festival honoring Saint Martin with traditional dances and processions in the Puno region.

festivals.description: Elite competition showcasing Peru's iconic Paso horse breed with demonstrations of traditional horsemanship and breeding.

festivals.name: Fiesta de la Vendimia

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Peru
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Early Settlement
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers arrive in the Andes and coastal regions, gradually developing agriculture.
items.text: The first humans to reach Peru crossed from Central America and began hunting megafauna across the Andean highlands and Pacific coast. Over millennia, these nomadic groups learned to domesticate llamas, alpacas, and potatoes in the highlands, while coastal communities mastered fishing and marine resource extraction. By 3,000 BC, permanent settlements emerged and early pottery appeared, marking the shift from pure hunter-gatherer societies to more complex communities with seasonal agriculture.

items.label: Early Horizon
items.summary: Monumental architecture and religious centers appear as societies grow more stratified.
items.text: The Norte Chico civilization built massive stone temples along the Peruvian coast without pottery or agriculture, supported instead by abundant marine resources. Simultaneously, the Chavín culture emerged in the northern highlands, spreading a unified religious ideology marked by jaguar symbolism and sophisticated textiles. These cultures developed the first large-scale irrigation systems and established trade networks that connected coast and highlands, laying the foundations for all future Peruvian civilizations.

items.label: Early Intermediate Period
items.summary: Regional kingdoms flourish with distinct pottery styles and territorial ambitions.
items.text: The Moche culture dominated Peru's north coast with remarkable ceramic art and monumental adobe pyramids like the Temple of the Sun. Simultaneously, the Nazca civilization of the southern coast created the famous Nazca Lines—vast geoglyphs etched into the desert—and produced exquisite polychrome pottery. Both cultures developed sophisticated irrigation systems that allowed them to thrive in arid environments, and both eventually declined due to environmental stress and climate change, leaving behind some of the Americas' most enigmatic archaeological mysteries.

items.label: Middle Horizon
items.summary: The Wari Empire expands militarily across the highlands, establishing the first true empire.
items.text: Rising from the south-central highlands, the Wari built the second-largest pre-Columbian city in South America and spread their influence through military conquest and religious conversion across much of Peru. They created an extensive road network that would later be perfected by the Inca and established administrative centers throughout conquered territories. The Wari also developed sophisticated textiles and pottery, and their empire's collapse around 1000 AD created a power vacuum that allowed regional kingdoms to reemerge.

items.label: Late Intermediate Period
items.summary: Competing regional kingdoms jockey for power in a fragmented landscape.
items.text: After the Wari collapse, the Chimú kingdom emerged on the north coast as one of Peru's most powerful pre-Inca civilizations, building the massive adobe city of Chan Chan. In the highlands, dozens of smaller kingdoms competed for resources and prestige, including the Inca who were still confined to the Cusco Valley. The Chinchaysuyu region in the far north remained independent under local lords, while the southern highlands fragmented into competing chiefdoms. This period saw intense warfare, sophisticated metallurgy, and the rise of regional maritime trade networks before the Inca began their imperial expansion.

items.label: Inca Empire Rise
items.summary: The Inca rapidly conquer and unify the Andean world into the hemisphere's largest empire.
items.text: Under Pachacuti, the Inca transformed from a regional power into continental masters, conquering the Chimú, Chinchaysuyu kingdoms, and territories stretching from present-day Colombia to Chile. They built Machu Picchu, thousands of miles of paved roads, and an administrative system so efficient it governed millions without a written language, using quipu knotted cords instead. The Inca employed a combination of military conquest, religious conversion, and strategic marriage alliances, deliberately relocating populations to prevent rebellion while integrating conquered peoples into their tribute system. By 1532, the Inca Empire at its zenith controlled perhaps twelve million people across one of history's most remarkable mountain civilizations.

items.label: Spanish Conquest
items.summary: Francisco Pizarro and a handful of conquistadors topple the Inca Empire in brutal conquest.
items.text: Pizarro arrived with just 168 men but found the Inca Empire weakened by a succession war between half-brothers Huáscar and Atahualpa, which he ruthlessly exploited. He captured Atahualpa at Cajamarca through treachery, executed him despite a ransom of gold, and installed a puppet emperor, systematically dismantling the Inca state from within. Spanish steel, horses, and crucially, diseases like smallpox and measles that killed millions, proved devastating against an enemy with no immunity. By 1572, the last independent Inca rebels were crushed in Vilcabamba, and Spain had seized control of the richest empire in South America.

items.label: Spanish Colonial Period
items.summary: Spain transforms Peru into a colonial extraction machine, mining silver and exploiting indigenous labor.
items.text: The Spanish established the Viceroyalty of Peru, with Lima as capital, and restructured society into rigid racial castes that privileged Europeans while enslaving and oppressing indigenous peoples. The discovery of silver at Potosí made Peru the wealthiest colony in the Americas, but mining operations devastated indigenous populations through brutal forced labor systems like the encomienda and mita. Catholic missionaries worked to eliminate indigenous religions while Spanish administrators reorganized communities, redistributed land, and imposed Spanish language and culture. Over two and a half centuries, millions of indigenous people died from disease, overwork, and maltreatment, while African slaves were brought to replace them in some regions, creating a complex multiracial colonial society.

items.label: Independence & Early Republic
items.summary: Peru gains independence but descends into decades of chaos, caudillo rule, and regional fragmentation.
items.text: José de San Martín declared Peru independent in 1821, but without strong institutions or unified leadership, the nation quickly fractured into competing regional strongmen called caudillos who fought over power and resources. Peru lost territory to neighbors in territorial wars, including the costly War of the Pacific (1879-1884) where Chile defeated Peru and Bolivia, annexing the rich nitrate regions of Antofagasta. The economy destabilized as silver mining declined and Spain's colonial trade monopolies disappeared, leaving Peru searching for new export products. This turbulent century saw hundreds of constitutions, countless coup d'états, and the rise and fall of military strongmen, with indigenous peoples continuing to suffer exploitation and marginalization.

items.label: Guano & Modernization Era
items.summary: Peru recovers economically through guano and sugar exports, modernizing cities and infrastructure despite persistent inequality.
items.text: Peru rebuilt its economy by exploiting guano deposits and developing large sugar and cotton plantations on the coast, attracting foreign investment and modernizing Lima with new railroads, ports, and architecture. The oligarchy that controlled these export industries grew wealthy while the majority of indigenous and mestizo populations remained impoverished and exploited. Chinese and Japanese indentured laborers arrived to work plantations, adding new ethnic diversity to Peru's complex society. Though Peru developed a more modern infrastructure and middle class during this period, wealth remained concentrated among a small elite, and indigenous peoples were marginalized in both economic and political systems.

items.label: Turbulent 20th Century
items.summary: Military coups, revolutionary movements, and authoritarianism plague Peru as it struggles toward democracy.
items.text: The Great Depression triggered the first of many military coups that would define Peru's twentieth century, as competing elites and leftist movements battled for control. The APRA party, founded by Víctor Raúl Haya de la Torre, emerged as a powerful force advocating for indigenous rights and social reform, though frequently suppressed by military regimes. Peru experienced military dictatorships, civilian democracies, and the rise of the leftist Shining Path guerrilla movement, which in the 1980s-90s launched a brutal insurgency that killed nearly 70,000 people. Hyperinflation, cocaine trafficking, economic collapse, and the Fight against terrorism defined much of this chaotic era, yet also produced cultural dynamism and indigenous political awakening.

items.label: Fujimori Era & Transition
items.summary: Authoritarian president Alberto Fujimori defeats the insurgency and modernizes the economy but dismantles democracy.
items.text: Fujimori won the 1990 election as a political outsider and shocked the nation by dissolving Congress in a 1992 autogolpe (self-coup), claiming emergency powers to defeat the Shining Path insurgency. He brutally suppressed the guerrillas through military operations and intelligence work, capturing Shining Path leader Abimael Guzmán and essentially winning the war by 1995. Though credited with restoring peace and economic stability through neoliberal reforms, Fujimori presided over systematic human rights violations, disappeared political opponents, and ruled as an authoritarian autocrat. His regime collapsed in 2000 amid corruption scandals and fraud allegations, forcing him to flee to Japan and sending Peru toward redemocratization.

items.label: Modern Peru
items.summary: Peru rebuilds democratic institutions while wrestling with corruption, inequality, and indigenous movements.
items.text: After Fujimori's fall, Peru restored civilian democracy and launched truth commissions to investigate the violence and human rights abuses of previous decades. Economic growth returned, driven by mining exports and free trade, lifting millions from poverty though benefits remained unequally distributed. Indigenous and mestizo movements have gained political voice, with indigenous candidates elected president and indigenous rights increasingly protected, though discrimination and inequality persist. Peru today remains a middle-income country battling corruption, drug trafficking, and political instability, yet maintains a vibrant civil society, rich cultural heritage, and growing recognition of its indigenous Andean and Amazonian roots.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Peru — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Café con leche is the morning ritual
items.text: Peruvians start their day with a large cup of weak espresso mixed with hot milk, often with a bread roll (pan). Ask for 'café con leche' at any café or even your hotel—it's cheap, ubiquitous, and fundamentally Peruvian.

items.label: Specialty coffee is a Lima thing
items.text: High-quality third-wave coffee shops are concentrated in Lima's Miraflores, Barranco, and San Isidro districts. Outside the capital, expect standard café or instant Nescafé; don't expect flat whites in Cusco or the Sacred Valley.

items.label: Café Café (Lima)
items.text: A reliable chain with good espresso drinks and pastries across Lima. It's tourist-friendly but actually serves competent coffee, unlike many tourist-targeted spots.

items.label: Café is served after meals, not with breakfast
items.text: Peruvians have coffee in the morning with bread, but 'café' (espresso) arrives after dinner. Don't expect automatic coffee service with your meal—order it specifically.

items.label: Ceviche
items.text: Raw fish cured in lime juice with red onions, cilantro, and chili. It's Peru's national dish—order it at lunch (never dinner) from a reputable restaurant near the coast, as freshness is critical.

items.label: Lomo saltado
items.text: Stir-fried beef with tomatoes, onions, and peppers, served with rice and fries. It's comfort food found everywhere and a reliable choice when uncertain.

items.label: Causa limeña
items.text: A cold terrine of mashed yellow potatoes layered with avocado, chicken, or seafood. It's a Lima specialty and perfect for warm days—look for it in coastal restaurants.

items.label: Ají de gallina
items.text: Shredded chicken in a creamy yellow chili sauce served over rice. It's homey and warming, especially good in the highlands, and typically inexpensive.

items.label: Anticuchos
items.text: Marinated and grilled beef heart skewers with potatoes and corn. Street vendors sell them late afternoon and evening—they're an authentic, affordable snack.

items.label: Papa a la huancaína
items.text: Boiled potatoes topped with a creamy yellow chili cheese sauce and olives. It's a classic starter and completely vegetarian, though some versions include chicken.

items.label: Chupe de camarones
items.text: A rich shrimp soup with potatoes, corn, and cream from Peru's coastal regions. It's hearty and often served with a raw egg yolk to stir in.

items.label: Menú del día is your budget friend
items.text: Most restaurants offer a fixed lunch menu (menú del día) with soup, main, dessert, and drink for 10–20 soles. It's cheaper than ordering à la carte and often better quality.

items.label: Ask what's fresh, not what's on the printed menu
items.text: In casual restaurants and at markets, ask 'Qué hay hoy?' (What do you have today?) rather than relying on the menu board. Fresh fish and seasonal items vary daily.

items.label: Portion sizes are large
items.text: A single main course often serves two people. Share dishes or ask for a media porción (half portion) if available, or expect significant leftovers.

items.label: Verify prices upfront at informal spots
items.text: At markets, street stalls, and small comedores (local eateries), no menu may be posted. Always ask the price before ordering to avoid surprises.

items.label: Agua sin gas is tap water (usually safe in cities)
items.text: In Lima and major cities, tap water is potable. Restaurants bring water automatically—specify 'sin gas' (still) or 'con gas' (sparkling) if you want bottled.

items.label: Mercados (markets) for authentic cheap eats
items.text: Seek out San Isidro or Santa Cruz markets for fresh ceviche, anticuchos, and regional dishes at 8–15 soles. Go at lunch when locals eat and food turns over quickly.

items.label: Comedores—tiny local eateries
items.text: Family-run hole-in-the-wall restaurants, often without signs, serve the menú del día to workers. They're cheap, genuine, and found in every neighborhood—ask locals to point you in.

items.label: Tourist restaurants in Sacred Valley charge premium prices
items.text: Restaurants with views of ruins or Ollantaytambo's fortress add 50–100% to prices. Head downhill into town or ask your hotel for local spots away from the tourist trail.

items.label: Lima's fine dining scene is world-class
items.text: Restaurants like Central, Maido, and Astrid y Gastón rank globally. Book weeks ahead; they're expensive but offer genuine haute Peruvian cuisine.

items.label: Avoid 'tourist menus' with photos and inflated prices
items.text: Laminated menus with pictures of food aimed at foreigners near major attractions signal tourist traps. Peruvians eat where there are no pictures and no English signs.

items.label: Barranco (Lima) for bohemian dining
items.text: This artsy neighborhood has honest mid-range restaurants, cevicherías, and cafés mixed with galleries and bookshops. It's touristy but genuinely vibrant, unlike the tourist monoculture of Plaza Mayor.

items.label: Pisco sour
items.text: Peru's national cocktail: grape brandy (pisco), lime juice, egg white, bitters, and sugar shaken into a frothy drink. Order it with ice; it's smooth but deceptively strong.

items.label: Chicha morada
items.text: A sweet, non-alcoholic drink made from purple corn, spices, and fruit. It's refreshing, unique to Peru, and served cold in markets and comedores.

items.label: Coca tea [té de coca]
items.text: Hot water steeped with coca leaves, served to ease altitude sickness in the highlands. It's mild, legal, and genuinely helps—order it at your Cusco hotel.

items.label: Peruvian beer is light and widely available
items.text: Cristal, Pilsen, and Cusqueña are the main brands—all light lagers. Local craft breweries exist in Lima, but industrial beer dominates outside the capital.

items.label: Inca Kola
items.text: A bright yellow, vanilla-flavored soda that's ubiquitous and uniquely Peruvian. It's overly sweet, but trying it is part of the experience.

items.label: Wine culture is growing but not traditional
items.text: Peru produces wine in southern valleys, but wine isn't as embedded in dining as in Chile or Argentina. Most restaurants offer basic wine lists; beer and pisco are more natural choices.

items.label: Street juice stands are fresh but potentially risky
items.text: Jugo stands blend tropical fruits daily—papaya, mango, passion fruit. The fruit is excellent, but water quality is uncertain; buy where there's high volume and locals queue.

items.label: Lunch (1–3 PM) is the main meal; dinner is light
items.text: Peruvians eat their largest meal at midday, then have a light supper (cena) around 8 PM. Restaurants fill at lunch; dinner service is often slow because fewer people eat heartily at night.

items.label: Tipping is expected but not automatic
items.text: In formal restaurants, leave 10% if service was good; in casual spots, 5% or loose change is fine. Credit card machines often ask for tips (propina)—decline if service was poor.

items.label: Peruvians eat slowly and socially
items.text: Meals are leisurely events, not rushed refuelings. Lingering for an hour or more is normal; rushing through signals rudeness. Don't expect quick table turnover.

items.label: Bread and butter arrive automatically—you'll be charged
items.text: Pan y mantequilla appear unsolicited at the table. If you eat it, you'll pay (usually 1–3 soles). Push it aside if you don't want the charge.

items.label: Say 'buen provecho' when locals are eating
items.text: It's the standard greeting in Peru equivalent to 'bon appétit.' Use it when passing someone eating or when served, and locals will respond warmly.

items.label: Ask for la cuenta (the bill) explicitly—it won't come unprompted
items.text: Servers leave you alone after eating unless you signal. Say 'la cuenta, por favor' or raise your hand—they won't rush you off.

sections.title: Markets & Street Food [Mercados y Comida Callejera]

items.label: Mercado Central (Lima)
items.text: Lima's largest food market is organized chaos with fresh produce, seafood, and cooked food stalls. Arrive before 1 PM for lunch; the atmosphere is intense but authentic.

items.label: Eat where locals queue, not where vendors call out
items.text: At markets, the busiest stalls have high turnover and lower food-safety risk. Avoid empty stalls and always watch your food being prepared.

items.label: Empanadas and pasteles
items.text: Hand-held fried pockets filled with meat, cheese, or vegetables sold by street vendors. They're cheap (2–4 soles), filling, and widely safe if eaten fresh from the fryer.

items.label: Choclo con queso
items.text: Roasted large-kernel corn (choclo) served with fresh cheese, sold at street stalls throughout the country. It's simple, nutritious, and a classic snack.

items.label: Ceviche from street vendors is riskier than restaurants
items.text: While market ceviche can be excellent, fish freshness and ice quality vary wildly. Save ceviche for established restaurants unless you're confident in the vendor's reputation.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Peru.

items.label: PeruRail & Inca Rail
items.text: Two main operators run scenic routes: PeruRail connects Cusco to Machu Picchu and Sacred Valley; Inca Rail offers similar routes with different schedules. Book weeks ahead during peak season (May–September) as trains fill quickly.

items.label: Hiking the Inca Trail alternative
items.text: Train tickets to Machu Picchu are expensive; consider taking a local train to Ollantaytambo then hiking or taking a minibus. Standard trains (not tourist trains) are cheaper but slower and less comfortable.
Strikes occasionally disrupt rail schedules, especially around labor disputes in June–July. Check operator websites 48 hours before travel and have backup transport plans.

items.label: Lima's Metro Line 1 & 2
items.text: Lima's metro is fast, safe, and cheap (around 2.5 soles ≈ $0.70 per ride). Use contactless cards at stations; cash is not accepted. Lines 1 and 2 cover major areas, though the network is limited compared to other capitals.

items.label: Inter-city buses
items.text: Companies like Oltursa, Cruz del Sur, and Movil Tours operate long-distance routes across Peru. Book online or at terminals; premium (cama) seats cost more but offer recline and meals on overnight trips.
City buses in Lima, Cusco, and Arequipa are crowded and frequent targets for pickpockets. Keep bags in front, avoid displaying phones or valuables, and use taxis/apps after dark.
Bus routes change without notice and signage is minimal. Ask locals or your hotel for current routes; apps like Google Maps are unreliable for real-time city bus updates in Peru.

items.label: Uber, Didi & Bolt
items.text: Ride apps operate in Lima, Cusco, Arequipa, and other major cities. Fares are transparent and safer than street taxis; apps are available 24/7 with driver ratings visible.
Unmetered street taxis in Peru are common and prone to overcharging foreigners. Agree on fares before entering or use ride apps; avoid hailing taxis on the street at night.

items.label: Official taxi stands
items.text: Hotels, airports, and official taxi ranks (paraderos) offer metered cabs or fixed rates. Tip is not expected but rounding up is appreciated; most taxis do not accept cards.
Kidnapping scams ('express kidnapping') are rare but documented in Lima. Use registered apps, avoid displaying cash, and do not accept rides from unmarked vehicles.

Road conditions outside Lima and Cusco are poor, with unpaved sections, landslides, and minimal signage. Hire a local driver or use buses instead; night driving is dangerous due to lack of streetlights and reckless drivers.

items.label: International Driving Permit
items.text: Bring an International Driving Permit (IDP) along with your passport and home license; Peru technically requires it. Fines for missing documents can reach 500+ soles.
Traffic police may stop drivers for minor infractions and demand on-the-spot fines. Remain calm, ask for a receipt, and avoid handing over your license; corruption is possible, though improving.

items.label: Car rental agencies
items.text: International companies (Hertz, Avis, Budget) operate at Lima and Cusco airports with better insurance options. Local agencies are cheaper but offer less protection; always purchase full coverage.

items.label: Jorge Chávez International (Lima) & Alejandro Velasco Astete (Cusco)
items.text: Peru's main hubs are Lima (connects to most international flights) and Cusco (gateway to Machu Picchu). Domestic airlines include LATAM, Avianca, Sky Airline, and Viva Air; book in advance for cheaper fares.

items.label: Airport transport
items.text: Use official taxi ranks at airport exits, book Uber/Didi via app, or arrange hotel pickup in advance. A taxi to central Lima costs 30–50 soles; airports are 15–25 km from city centers.

Baggage handlers and unofficial 'helpers' at airports may demand tips for assistance; politely decline and keep luggage in sight. Overpacking can result in excess fees, and security screening is slow during peak hours.

items.label: Altitude in Cusco
items.text: Cusco airport sits at 3,400 m (11,150 ft); take it slow upon arrival to avoid altitude sickness. Many flights from Lima arrive mid-afternoon, giving you time to acclimatize before activities.

items.label: Lake Titicaca tours
items.text: Boats from Puno (Peru) and Copacabana (Bolivia) cross the world's highest navigable lake (3,812 m). Tours to the Uros Floating Islands and Taquile Island operate daily; book through hotels or agencies.

items.label: Amazon river cruises
items.text: Iquitos and Puerto Maldonado are the main access points; companies like Delfin and Anakonda offer multi-day cruises. Budget lodges cost $100–200/night; luxury cruises exceed $1,000, often booked from home.

Private boat operators on lakes and rivers vary widely in safety standards. Choose companies with established reputations, verify life jackets and safety gear, and avoid traveling with unmarked vessels.

items.label: Tourist Bracelet (Pulsera Turística) in Cusco
items.text: A combined ticket grants entry to 16 regional sites including Sacsayhuamán, Pisac ruins, and Ollantaytambo for 130 soles (about $35). Valid 10 days; purchase at the Cusco visitor center or site entrances.

items.label: Machu Picchu tickets
items.text: Book online at machupicchu.gob.pe weeks ahead; on-site sales are limited and sell out by mid-morning. Tickets include a 3–4 hour entry window; arrive early to avoid crowds and heat.
Some tour operators sell counterfeit or duplicate tickets online. Purchase directly from official sites or through reputable agencies; screenshots and email confirmations are your receipt.

items.label: Lima City Pass
items.text: Tourism bureaus offer multi-attraction passes covering museums and sites, though individual tickets often cost less. Calculate costs per site before purchasing a bundle.

items.label: Local SIM providers
items.text: Movistar, Claro, and Entel are the major carriers; buy SIM cards at airports, kiosks, or convenience stores for 5–20 soles. Prepaid data plans are cheap (10–30 GB for $10–20/month) with decent 4G coverage in cities.

items.label: WiFi availability
items.text: Most hotels, hostels, and cafes offer free WiFi, though speeds vary widely. Peru has widespread internet in urban centers but spotty coverage in rural areas and the Amazon.
Public WiFi networks, especially at airports and malls, are not encrypted; avoid banking or sharing sensitive data on them. Use a VPN if accessing personal accounts on public networks.

items.label: Offline maps
items.text: Download Google Maps or Maps.me offline for regions with poor signal, especially when trekking or visiting remote areas. Data-heavy apps like translation tools should be cached before travel.
